```markdown
---
name: developer-agent-rules-code-review-insight-report
description: >
  Turn raw notes into insights for code reviews: actionable patterns from messy inputs. Produces a copy-ready insight report. Triggers: "code reviews insight-report", /developer-agent-rules-code-review-insight-report.
metadata:
  category: developer
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /developer-agent-rules-code-review-insight-report

Turn raw notes into insights for code reviews: actionable patterns from messy inputs. Produces a copy-ready insight report. Triggers: "code reviews insight-report", /developer-agent-rules-code-review-insight-report.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when there's a body of review evidence to mine - a quarter of PR comments, a set of post-merge defects, or the review history of agent-written PRs specifically - and the team wants to know what review is actually catching, what it's missing, and what should change: checklist updates, new CI rules that automate recurring comments, or targeted rules for agent-authored code.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The corpus: exported PR review comments, approval/rejection outcomes, revert and hotfix history, bug tickets that link back to PRs
- Authorship split: which PRs were agent-generated vs human-written (labels, commit trailers, or bot accounts)
- Current review checklist/rubric and CI ruleset, so findings can be classified as "already covered but missed" vs "not covered"
- Time window and any process changes inside it (new linter, new team members, model upgrade) that would confound trends

## Workflow

1. Classify a sample of review comments (or all, if tooling permits) into: correctness defects, missing/weak tests, security concerns, design/architecture pushback, readability/naming, and style-nits-a-linter-should-own. Count per category and per authorship type.
2. Cross-reference escapes: for each post-merge defect, find its PR and answer - was the defect visible in the diff, did any reviewer comment near it, was the file even opened (large-diff blindness)? This yields the miss taxonomy: not-visible-in-diff, visible-but-unremarked, remarked-but-merged-anyway.
3. For agent-authored PRs, additionally scan for the known red-flag patterns and count them: overly broad try/except that swallows errors, tests weakened or deleted to get green, hallucinated APIs caught (or not) by reviewers, plausible-but-wrong edge-case handling, TODO-comments standing in for logic, dependency bumps smuggled into feature diffs.
4. Find the automation candidates: any comment type appearing more than a handful of times that a machine could enforce (import order, missing error wrapping, forbidden patterns) - every one of these is reviewer attention leaking away from the categories machines can't judge.
5. Correlate defect escapes with PR attributes: diff size, files touched, review turnaround, single vs multiple reviewers. Report the correlations you actually found (with counts), not folklore.
6. Convert findings into three action lists: add-to-CI (automate the repetitive), add-to-checklist (human attention items, especially agent-PR red flags), and process changes (size caps, risk-tiered review) - each tied to the evidence row that justifies it.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.
- Report patterns at team level; never rank or name individual reviewers' miss rates - that turns a process instrument into a performance weapon and dries up honest data.

## Output format

**Objective** - corpus, window, and the decision the report feeds.

**Inputs used** - PR/comment/defect counts, authorship split method.

**Insight report**

- Themes: comment-category distribution; miss taxonomy with counts; agent-PR red-flag frequencies vs human baseline
- Evidence: 2-3 anonymized examples per theme (comment excerpt or diff hunk with identifiers stripped)
- Opportunities: automation candidates with the CI rule each implies; checklist gaps with proposed wording
- Actions: add-to-CI / add-to-checklist / process-change lists, each item citing its evidence

**Risks / assumptions** - sampling bias, authorship labeling accuracy, confounds inside the window.

**Next action** - usually: land the top two CI automations, then re-measure comment mix next quarter.

## Do

- Distinguish "reviewer missed it" from "diff made it invisible" - the fixes differ (checklist vs size caps/render tooling)
- Treat every recurring nit as a CI rule waiting to be written; human review time is the scarce resource being defended
- Baseline agent-PR findings against human-PR findings - the delta is the story, not the raw count
- Include remarked-but-merged-anyway cases; they indicate approval pressure, not detection failure

## Don't

- Don't report comment volume as review quality - a rubber-stamp culture and a nit-storm culture both mislead
- Don't name-and-shame reviewers or authors; aggregate or anonymize
- Don't claim a trend across a window containing a linter rollout or model upgrade without splitting the data at that boundary
- Don't propose checklist items no one can check in under a minute; unusable checklists get skipped silently

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Comment taxonomy counts are real counts, not impressions
- [ ] Miss taxonomy separates visibility failures from attention failures
- [ ] Agent-vs-human deltas shown with baselines
- [ ] Every recommended action cites its evidence row
- [ ] No individual performance rankings anywhere in the report
```

For agent-authored PRs, additionally scan for the known red-flag patterns and count them: overly broad try/except that swallows errors, tests weakened or deleted to get green, hallucinated APIs caught (or not) by reviewers, plausible-but-wrong edge-case handling, TODO-comments standing in for logic, dependency bumps smuggled into feature diffs.

5. Correlate defect escapes with PR attributes: diff size, files touched, review turnaround, single vs multiple reviewers. Report the correlations you actually found (with counts), not folklore.
6. Convert findings into three action lists: add-to-CI (automate the repetitive), add-to-checklist (human attention items, especially agent-PR red flags), and process changes (size caps, risk-tiered review) - each tied to the evidence row that justifies it.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.
- Report patterns at team level; never rank or name individual reviewers' miss rates - that turns a process instrument into a performance weapon and dries up honest data.

## Output format

**Objective** - corpus, window, and the decision the report feeds.

**Inputs used** - PR/comment/defect counts, authorship split method.

**Insight report**

- Themes: comment-category distribution; miss taxonomy with counts; agent-PR red-flag frequencies vs human baseline
- Evidence: 2-3 anonymized examples per theme (comment excerpt or diff hunk with identifiers stripped)
- Opportunities: automation candidates with the CI rule each implies; checklist gaps with proposed wording
- Actions: add-to-CI / add-to-checklist / process-change lists, each item citing its evidence

**Risks / assumptions** - sampling bias, authorship labeling accuracy, confounds inside the window.

**Next action** - usually: land the top two CI automations, then re-measure comment mix next quarter.

## Do

- Distinguish "reviewer missed it" from "diff made it invisible" - the fixes differ (checklist vs size caps/render tooling)
- Treat every recurring nit as a CI rule waiting to be written; human review time is the scarce resource being defended
- Baseline agent-PR findings against human-PR findings - the delta is the story, not the raw count
- Include remarked-but-merged-anyway cases; they indicate approval pressure, not detection failure

## Don't

- Don't report comment volume as review quality - a rubber-stamp culture and a nit-storm culture both mislead
- Don't name-and-shame reviewers or authors; aggregate or anonymize
- Don't claim a trend across a window containing a linter rollout or model upgrade without splitting the data at that boundary
- Don't propose checklist items no one can check in under a minute; unusable checklists get skipped silently

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Comment taxonomy counts are real counts, not impressions
- [ ] Miss taxonomy separates visibility failures from attention failures
- [ ] Agent-vs-human deltas shown with baselines
- [ ] Every recommended action cites its evidence row
- [ ] No individual performance rankings anywhere in the report

# /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-research-brief

Build a research brief for refactor plans: sourced questions, findings, and implications. Produces a copy-ready research brief. Triggers: "refactor plans research-brief", /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-research-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team faces a real refactor (or a backlog of them) and needs to choose an execution approach before writing the plan: incremental strangler-fig versus a coordinated rewrite, branch-by-abstraction, mechanical codemods, agent-executed changes under rules, or plain human hands. It weighs those approaches against the codebase's actual conditions - coverage, deploy cadence, dependency shape - and names what to verify in the repo before committing. If the approach is already chosen and the question is whether it is safe, use the experiment-plan skill.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- What is being refactored and why now: the pain (change amplification, defect cluster, migration prerequisite), not just "the code is ugly"
- Test coverage reality on the affected paths - reported numbers and how trusted they are
- Deploy cadence and rollback capability: continuous deployment changes the risk math versus quarterly releases
- Dependency shape: how many callers, dynamic access patterns (reflection, string-keyed dispatch) that mechanical tools miss
- Team capacity, feature-freeze tolerance, and whether agent execution is on the table

## Workflow

1. Frame the decision in one sentence including the "why now" - a refactor justified only by aesthetics should be named as such, since the honest option may be to not do it.
2. Enumerate the candidate approaches, always including "don't refactor; contain instead": incremental extraction behind existing tests, strangler-fig with parallel-run verification, branch-by-abstraction for long-lived changes on a shared trunk, codemod/mechanical transformation for large uniform changes, agent-executed refactor under strict rules of engagement, and full rewrite (stated with its real historical failure rate in mind, not romanticized).
3. For each approach, state the preconditions it depends on and its characteristic failure mode: big-bang branches rot into unmergeable conflict; codemods silently miss dynamic call sites; strangler-fig stalls half-migrated when priorities shift, leaving two systems forever; agent execution produces plausible-but-wrong API substitutions and giant diffs when uncapped.
4. Map approaches against the gathered constraints - thin coverage disqualifies anything that cannot be verified step-by-step; slow rollback disqualifies high-blast-radius steps; heavy dynamic dispatch disqualifies pure codemods.
5. List repo-specific facts to verify before deciding, each with a concrete check: actual branch coverage on target paths (run the coverage tool, do not trust the README), the real caller graph (grep plus runtime traces for dynamic usage), and whether the suite catches a deliberately introduced behavior change (a quick mutation spot-check).
6. Recommend one approach or an explicit sequence (e.g., characterization tests, then codemod, then manual cleanup), with the smallest reversible first step and the evidence that would reverse the choice.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - the refactor decision in one sentence, including why now.

**Codebase conditions** - coverage reality, deploy/rollback, dependency shape, capacity.

**Options landscape** - table: approach, preconditions it needs, cost, characteristic failure mode.

**Findings per option** - fit against this codebase's conditions, not generic textbook pros and cons.

**Verify before deciding** - repo-specific facts with the exact check for each (coverage run, caller-graph grep, mutation spot-check).

**Implications** - what the front-runner commits the team to: freeze windows, parallel-run infrastructure, review load, agent rules of engagement.

**Recommendation** - one approach or sequence, smallest reversible first step, reversal condition.

**Risks / assumptions** - clearly separated, including the do-nothing cost.

**Next action** - usually running the verification checks.

## Do

- Include "contain, don't refactor" as a live option with its own cost line
- Tie every approach to the precondition it needs - strangler-fig without a seam, or agent execution without coverage, is a named mismatch
- Make each verification step executable: the command or query, not "assess coverage"
- Treat agent execution as an approach with rules-of-engagement requirements, not a free accelerant on any other option

## Don't

- Don't recommend a rewrite without stating what happens to incoming changes during it
- Don't trust reported coverage numbers - distinguish "lines executed" from "behavior asserted" and say which one the repo has
- Don't present all options as equally viable when the constraints have already eliminated some; eliminate them on the page
- Don't cite industry success rates or benchmark figures you cannot source; argue from this repo's conditions

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The "why now" is stated and the do-nothing option is costed
- [ ] Every approach lists its preconditions and failure mode
- [ ] Verification steps are concrete commands/checks against this repo
- [ ] The recommendation includes a smallest reversible first step and a reversal condition
- [ ] Agent execution, if considered, carries explicit rules-of-engagement requirements
```

```markdown
---
name: developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-scorecard
description: >
  Create a decision scorecard for refactor plans: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "refactor plans scorecard", /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-scorecard.
metadata:
  category: developer
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-scorecard

Create a decision scorecard for refactor plans: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "refactor plans scorecard", /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-scorecard.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team wants to grade how it currently plans and executes refactors against an explicit safety rubric - auditing recent refactor PRs rather than trading impressions. Good moments: before expanding agent-executed refactoring, after a refactor-caused incident, or as a periodic health check. It can also grade a single proposed refactor plan before execution. The output is a scored rubric with quoted evidence, the weakest dimension diagnosed, one fix, and a re-score date.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- A sample of recent refactor PRs (5-15), or the single refactor plan to be graded
- Diff sizes, review durations, and whether test files changed within those PRs
- Any reverts, regressions, or incidents traced to sampled refactors
- Who executed each: human, agent, or codemod
- The team's rollback capability, since it caps how much refactor risk is tolerable

## Workflow

1. Fix and state the sample: which PRs, what period, how selected. If grading a plan rather than history, say so - the rubric applies to both but the evidence differs.
2. Score each dimension 0-3 with anchored definitions: **coverage-first discipline** (0 = refactors land on untested paths, 3 = characterization tests demonstrably precede risky changes), **behavior preservation** (0 = refactors routinely bundle behavior changes, 3 = strictly separated, with parity checks where it matters), **commit and PR granularity** (0 = monolithic diffs, 3 = each PR reviewable line-by-line, mechanical steps isolated), **test integrity** (0 = assertions weakened or tests deleted to reach green, 3 = test changes limited to mechanical updates and justified in the PR description), **rollback readiness** (0 = no plan, 3 = each step independently revertible and the revert path stated), **post-refactor verification** (0 = "CI passed", 3 = behavior/performance parity checked where relevant).
3. Weight by exposure: teams running agent-executed refactors should up-weight test integrity and granularity, since those are exactly the dimensions agents fail quietly.
4. Gather evidence per dimension from the actual diffs - quote the weakened assertion, name the 4,000-line PR, link the revert. A score without a quote is a guess.
5. Compute per-dimension and weighted overall scores; flag any 0-1 score on test integrity or behavior preservation as a critical failure that the average must not launder.
6. Diagnose the weakest dimension in a short paragraph and propose exactly one fix - usually a mechanical control (diff cap, protected test paths, required parity check) rather than a plea for care.
7. Set the re-score: same rubric, same sampling method, stated date.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - what is being graded (practice over a period, or one plan) and why now.

**Sample** - PRs/plan reviewed, period, selection method, executor mix (human/agent/codemod).

**Rubric & scores** - table: dimension, weight, 0/3 anchors, score, one-line quoted evidence.

**Critical-failure flags** - any dimension at 0-1 that overrides the average, stated plainly.

**Overall grade** - weighted score plus a one-line interpretation ("safe to extend agent scope" / "fix integrity first").

**Weakest dimension deep dive** - mechanism and 2-3 cited examples.

**One fix** - a mechanical control with an owner, not an exhortation.

**Risks / assumptions** - sampling limits, attribution uncertainty.

**Next action** - re-score date and method.

## Do

- Anchor 0 and 3 for every dimension so a second grader lands within a point
- Quote diffs for test-integrity findings - the changed assertion line itself
- Let a critical failure on test integrity or behavior preservation cap the overall grade regardless of other scores
- Prefer fixes that are enforceable by tooling (CI diff caps, CODEOWNERS on test dirs) over documented intentions

## Don't

- Don't average a 0 in test integrity into a comfortable B-minus; say the practice is unsafe
- Don't grade the team's refactoring philosophy - grade the sampled PRs as they landed
- Don't treat "no incidents" as a 3 on behavior preservation when detection (coverage, monitoring) is weak; score the evidence, note the blind spot
- Don't propose a fix-everything roadmap; one dimension, one control, one re-score

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Sample and selection method precede any score
- [ ] Every dimension has anchored 0/3 definitions and quoted evidence
- [ ] Critical-failure override is applied where warranted
- [ ] Weights reflect executor mix (agent-heavy practice up-weights integrity and granularity)
- [ ] Exactly one mechanical fix and a dated re-score are included
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-scorecard or describe the task from the skill description.

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team wants to pilot a refactoring practice before trusting it broadly - most commonly, letting a coding agent execute refactors on low-risk modules under strict rules, or trialing a planned-refactor discipline (coverage-first, behavior-preserving steps, small commits) on one subsystem. The output is a bounded pilot with pre-flight checks, rules of engagement, and a hard stop rule. It is not the refactor plan itself; it is the plan to find out whether the practice is safe here.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Candidate modules: churn rate, test coverage, number of dependents, clarity of ownership
- Current test suite health: coverage on the target paths, runtime, flake rate
- Who or what executes the refactor (agent, tooling, humans) and who reviews
- Deployment and rollback story: how fast can a bad refactor be reverted in production
- What "refactor debt" prompted this - so success criteria measure the right relief

## Workflow

1. Select pilot targets for safety, not for need: modules with strong test coverage, low blast radius, few dependents, and a clear owner. The gnarly legacy module that "most needs refactoring" is the worst possible pilot subject.
2. Run pre-flight measurements: line and branch coverage on the target, test runtime, and current defect rate. Where coverage is thin, add characterization tests that pin existing behavior *before* the pilot starts - refactoring untested code is not an experiment, it is gambling.
3. Write the rules of engagement, verbatim, for whoever executes: behavior-preserving changes only; no public API or schema changes; every commit leaves the suite green; a maximum diff size per PR (small enough that a reviewer actually reads every line); refactor PRs never mixed with feature or bugfix changes; and - critical for agents - tests may not be modified, weakened, skipped, or deleted to make a change pass.
4. Define success criteria a skeptic would accept: zero behavior regressions traced to pilot PRs, every PR reviewed line-by-line within the team's normal review budget, and a reviewer-confidence check after each tranche.
5. Define the stop rule: any production incident traced to a pilot refactor, any discovered test modification by the executor, or any PR too large to review halts the pilot immediately pending a retro.
6. Structure the pilot in tranches (e.g., one module at a time) with a checkpoint review between tranches, so trust is extended incrementally rather than all at once.
7. Plan verification beyond "tests pass": diff-level review for sneaky behavior changes, and where feasible a before/after check on performance-sensitive paths.
8. Assemble the plan in the output format, copy-paste-ready.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - what practice is being piloted and what trust decision the pilot informs.

**Pilot targets** - modules chosen, with the safety rationale for each (coverage, blast radius, ownership).

**Pre-flight baseline** - coverage, suite runtime, flake rate on targets; characterization-test gaps to close first.

**Rules of engagement** - the verbatim ruleset for the executor, including the no-test-modification rule and max diff size.

**Success criteria** - regression count, reviewability, reviewer confidence; each with how it is checked.

**Stop rule** - the specific events that halt the pilot instantly.

**Tranche schedule** - order of modules, checkpoint reviews between tranches.

**Decision criteria** - what result extends the practice, what result adjusts it, what result ends it.

**Risks / assumptions** - e.g., pilot modules being unrepresentative of the code that actually needs refactoring.

**Next action** - usually the coverage/characterization-test pre-flight.

## Do

- Insist on coverage-first: characterization tests before the first refactor commit, not after
- Cap diff size so every pilot PR is genuinely line-by-line reviewable - giant green-tests diffs are where regressions hide
- Keep refactor commits mechanically separable: rename in one commit, extract in another, never "refactor + fix" together
- Treat any executor edit to test files as a pilot-stopping event, not a review comment

## Don't

- Don't pilot on the code with the worst coverage just because it hurts the most
- Don't accept "the suite is green" as proof of behavior preservation when the executor also touched the assertions
- Don't let the pilot's refactor PRs carry drive-by feature changes; they contaminate the measurement and the review
- Don't extend agent autonomy mid-pilot because early tranches went well - finish the pilot, then decide

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Pilot targets are justified by safety (coverage, blast radius), not by refactoring need
- [ ] The rules of engagement explicitly forbid modifying, skipping, or deleting tests to get to green
- [ ] A maximum reviewable diff size is stated
- [ ] The stop rule names concrete halting events, including test tampering
- [ ] Pre-flight characterization-test work is scheduled before the first refactor commit
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-experiment-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

  Turn raw notes into insights for refactor plans: actionable patterns from messy inputs. Produces a copy-ready insight report. Triggers: "refactor plans insight-report", /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-insight-report.
metadata:

# /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-insight-report

Turn raw notes into insights for refactor plans: actionable patterns from messy inputs. Produces a copy-ready insight report. Triggers: "refactor plans insight-report", /developer-agent-rules-refactor-plan-insight-report.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when refactors - human- or agent-executed - have been landing for a while and the team wants an evidence-based read on how safe and effective they have actually been. Input is real history: refactor-labeled PRs, revert commits, incident links, review threads. The report answers three questions: did refactors preserve behavior, were they reviewable, and did test integrity survive them. If no refactors have shipped yet, use the experiment-plan skill instead.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- The set of refactor PRs in the review period (labels, titles, or a hand-picked list), with diff sizes and review durations if available
- Reverts, hotfixes, or incidents traced to any of those PRs
- Which refactors were agent-executed versus human-executed
- Whether test files were changed in the same PRs as the refactors they validate
- Review threads or retro notes where reviewers voiced concerns

## Workflow

1. Fix the evidence base: list the PRs, incidents, and threads reviewed and the period covered; note refactors that may be missing from the sample (unlabeled, squashed).
2. Assess behavior preservation: for each sampled PR, was it advertised as behavior-preserving, and did anything downstream contradict that - reverts, regression bugs, "this also changed X" review comments, incident timelines.
3. Assess reviewability: chart the diff-size distribution and review depth. Flag the rubber-stamp signal - large diffs approved in minutes with no comments - because that is where regressions pass unread.
4. Audit test integrity, the highest-value check for agent-executed refactors: in each PR, were tests modified alongside the code they cover? Distinguish legitimate mechanical updates (renamed symbols) from weakened assertions, broadened tolerances, added skips, or deleted test cases. Quote the specific diffs.
5. Check for scope contamination: refactor PRs that also slipped in behavior changes, dependency bumps, or "while I was here" fixes - these break both review and bisection.
6. Compare agent-executed and human-executed refactors on the same dimensions, if both exist, and note any difference in failure signature (agents tend toward plausible-but-wrong API usage and assertion weakening; humans toward scope creep).
7. Rank findings by evidence strength and blast radius, then recommend two or three changes - rules-of-engagement fixes, diff caps, test-file protection - each with an owner and a confirming signal.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - the question this report answers about the team's refactoring practice.

**Evidence reviewed** - PR count, period, incident sources, known gaps in the sample.

**Behavior-preservation findings** - regressions and reverts traced to refactors, with PR references; or a clean bill with the caveat of detection limits.

**Reviewability findings** - diff-size distribution, review-depth observations, rubber-stamp incidents.

**Test-integrity findings** - every case of tests changed alongside refactored code, classified as mechanical vs weakening, with quoted evidence.

**Scope-contamination findings** - refactor PRs carrying unrelated changes.

**Agent vs human comparison** - if applicable, same dimensions side by side.

**Recommendations** - 2-3 changes with owner and confirming signal.

**Risks / assumptions** - sample gaps, attribution uncertainty (was the incident really the refactor?).

**Next action** - the single highest-leverage change.

## Do

- Quote the specific PR and, for test-integrity findings, the specific assertion that changed - this is the difference between a finding and an accusation
- Treat "large diff, fast approval, zero comments" as a process finding even when nothing broke
- Distinguish mechanical test updates from weakened tests explicitly; conflating them destroys the report's credibility
- Note detection limits honestly: absence of found regressions is weak evidence when coverage is thin

## Don't

- Don't blame individuals or "the agent" rhetorically; report mechanisms and fixes
- Don't invent regression counts or review-time statistics not present in the data
- Don't score a refactor as safe solely because CI stayed green - green CI with edited assertions proves nothing
- Don't bury the test-integrity section; for agent-executed refactors it is the headline

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every finding cites a specific PR, commit, or thread
- [ ] Test-file changes within refactor PRs were audited and classified
- [ ] The rubber-stamp signal (diff size vs review depth) was checked
- [ ] Agent- and human-executed refactors are compared when both exist
- [ ] Recommendations are process changes with owners, not exhortations to be careful
```

```markdown
---
name: developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-research-brief
description: >
  Build a research brief for test strategies: sourced questions, findings, and implications. Produces a copy-ready research brief. Triggers: "test strategies research-brief", /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-research-brief.
metadata:
  category: developer
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-research-brief

Build a research brief for test strategies: sourced questions, findings, and implications. Produces a copy-ready research brief. Triggers: "test strategies research-brief", /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-research-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team is designing or redesigning the test approach for a codebase or a major feature and needs the option space weighed before writing tests or buying CI minutes: classic pyramid versus integration-heavy shapes, contract testing between services, snapshot testing, property-based testing where it pays, and how much E2E is worth its flake. The brief argues from the system's shape and its escaped-defect history, and it treats every testing school's claims with the same skepticism - including the pyramid's.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- System shape: monolith, services, frontend-heavy, data pipeline - and where its risk concentrates (money paths, data integrity, cross-service contracts)
- Escaped-defect history: what actually broke in the last few quarters and at what boundary
- Current suite and its pain: runtime, flake, what developers trust and ignore
- CI budget and team discipline realities (will anyone maintain contract fixtures? who reviews snapshot updates?)
- Whether coding agents write tests here, and under what review

## Workflow

1. Frame the decision by risk concentration: "checkout and refunds must not break; the admin panel may" is a strategy input; "increase quality" is not. Name the top defect boundary from history.
2. Enumerate the candidate shapes with the current suite as baseline: unit-heavy pyramid, integration-heavy shape (fewer mocks, real wiring, slower but truer), consumer-driven contract tests for service boundaries, E2E smoke sets, snapshot testing for UI surface, property-based testing for parsers/serializers/money math and other algebraic code.
3. State each approach's honest tradeoff mechanically: unit tests give fast feedback but over-mocked units verify the mock and pass while the system is broken; integration tests catch wiring but need infrastructure discipline; E2E is the only level that tests what users experience and the first level to go flaky and get ignored; snapshots are cheap to write and cheaper to rubber-stamp on update; property-based tests find edge cases humans do not write but only fit code with statable properties; contract tests catch exactly the cross-service escapes - if both sides keep the contracts honest.
4. Weigh against constraints: CI budget caps the E2E appetite; low fixture-maintenance discipline argues against contract testing however well it fits; agent-written tests shift the risk toward high-volume low-assertion tests, which argues for assertion-quality review rather than more test count.
5. List what to verify in this repo before deciding, each with a concrete check: re-map the last N escaped defects to the cheapest level that would have caught them; time the current suite's feedback per level; run a mutation-testing spot-check on one "well-covered" module to test whether the existing tests catch deliberate breakage.
6. Recommend a target shape as investment priorities (first hundred hours go here, not a percentage dogma), plus the two policies every strategy needs stated: a flaky-test policy (quarantine with a fix SLA, never silent retries) and a coverage stance (changed-line coverage as a floor-finder; aggregate percentage never a goal).

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - the strategy question, framed by risk concentration and the top defect boundary.

**System & history** - shape, risk paths, escaped-defect boundaries, current suite pain.

**Options landscape** - table: approach, what it catches, feedback speed, maintenance/discipline cost, characteristic failure mode.

**Findings per approach** - fit against this system's boundaries and this team's discipline, not textbook advocacy.

**Verify before deciding** - the escape re-mapping, per-level feedback timing, and the mutation spot-check, each with method.

**Implications** - what the recommended shape demands: infrastructure, fixture ownership, review norms for snapshots and agent-written tests.

**Recommendation** - investment priorities plus the two mandatory policies (flake quarantine with SLA; coverage-as-floor stance).

**Risks / assumptions** - separated clearly, including where history is too thin to weigh.

**Next action** - usually the escaped-defect re-mapping.

## Do

- Let the escaped-defect boundary, not a diagram from a conference talk, pick where the next hundred test-hours go
- State the over-mocking failure mode explicitly when recommending unit-heavy shapes
- Include the mutation spot-check; it is the cheapest way to expose assertion-free "coverage" before betting on it
- Address agent-written tests directly: volume is easy, assertion quality is the review target

## Don't

- Don't prescribe pyramid ratios as universal truth; the right shape follows the system's failure boundaries
- Don't recommend contract testing without naming who maintains each side's contracts - unowned contracts rot into false confidence

# /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-research-brief

Build a research brief for test strategies: sourced questions, findings, and implications. Produces a copy-ready research brief. Triggers: "test strategies research-brief", /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-research-brief.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team is designing or redesigning the test approach for a codebase or a major feature and needs the option space weighed before writing tests or buying CI minutes: classic pyramid versus integration-heavy shapes, contract testing between services, snapshot testing, property-based testing where it pays, and how much E2E is worth its flake. The brief argues from the system's shape and its escaped-defect history, and it treats every testing school's claims with the same skepticism - including the pyramid's.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- System shape: monolith, services, frontend-heavy, data pipeline - and where its risk concentrates (money paths, data integrity, cross-service contracts)
- Escaped-defect history: what actually broke in the last few quarters and at what boundary
- Current suite and its pain: runtime, flake, what developers trust and ignore
- CI budget and team discipline realities (will anyone maintain contract fixtures? who reviews snapshot updates?)
- Whether coding agents write tests here, and under what review

## Workflow

1. Frame the decision by risk concentration: "checkout and refunds must not break; the admin panel may" is a strategy input; "increase quality" is not. Name the top defect boundary from history.
2. Enumerate the candidate shapes with the current suite as baseline: unit-heavy pyramid, integration-heavy shape (fewer mocks, real wiring, slower but truer), consumer-driven contract tests for service boundaries, E2E smoke sets, snapshot testing for UI surface, property-based testing for parsers/serializers/money math and other algebraic code.
3. State each approach's honest tradeoff mechanically: unit tests give fast feedback but over-mocked units verify the mock and pass while the system is broken; integration tests catch wiring but need infrastructure discipline; E2E is the only level that tests what users experience and the first level to go flaky and get ignored; snapshots are cheap to write and cheaper to rubber-stamp on update; property-based tests find edge cases humans do not write but only fit code with statable properties; contract tests catch exactly the cross-service escapes - if both sides keep the contracts honest.
4. Weigh against constraints: CI budget caps the E2E appetite; low fixture-maintenance discipline argues against contract testing however well it fits; agent-written tests shift the risk toward high-volume low-assertion tests, which argues for assertion-quality review rather than more test count.
5. List what to verify in this repo before deciding, each with a concrete check: re-map the last N escaped defects to the cheapest level that would have caught them; time the current suite's feedback per level; run a mutation-testing spot-check on one "well-covered" module to test whether the existing tests catch deliberate breakage.
6. Recommend a target shape as investment priorities (first hundred hours go here, not a percentage dogma), plus the two policies every strategy needs stated: a flaky-test policy (quarantine with a fix SLA, never silent retries) and a coverage stance (changed-line coverage as a floor-finder; aggregate percentage never a goal).

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - the strategy question, framed by risk concentration and the top defect boundary.

**System & history** - shape, risk paths, escaped-defect boundaries, current suite pain.

**Options landscape** - table: approach, what it catches, feedback speed, maintenance/discipline cost, characteristic failure mode.

**Findings per approach** - fit against this system's boundaries and this team's discipline, not textbook advocacy.

**Verify before deciding** - the escape re-mapping, per-level feedback timing, and the mutation spot-check, each with method.

**Implications** - what the recommended shape demands: infrastructure, fixture ownership, review norms for snapshots and agent-written tests.

**Recommendation** - investment priorities plus the two mandatory policies (flake quarantine with SLA; coverage-as-floor stance).

**Risks / assumptions** - separated clearly, including where history is too thin to weigh.

**Next action** - usually the escaped-defect re-mapping.

## Do

- Let the escaped-defect boundary, not a diagram from a conference talk, pick where the next hundred test-hours go
- State the over-mocking failure mode explicitly when recommending unit-heavy shapes
- Include the mutation spot-check; it is the cheapest way to expose assertion-free "coverage" before betting on it
- Address agent-written tests directly: volume is easy, assertion quality is the review target

## Don't

- Don't prescribe pyramid ratios as universal truth; the right shape follows the system's failure boundaries
- Don't recommend contract testing without naming who maintains each side's contracts - unowned contracts rot into false confidence
- Don't treat coverage percentage as a strategy goal anywhere in the brief; it measures execution, not verification
- Don't cite defect-detection statistics or industry ratios you cannot source; the repo's own escape history is the evidence

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Risk concentration and the top defect boundary are named and drive the weighting
- [ ] Every approach carries a mechanical failure mode, including the fashionable ones
- [ ] Verification includes escape re-mapping and a mutation spot-check on a "covered" module
- [ ] The recommendation is investment priorities plus explicit flake and coverage policies
- [ ] Agent-written-test review implications are addressed if agents write tests here

```markdown
---
name: developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-scorecard
description: >
  Create a decision scorecard for test strategies: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "test strategies scorecard", /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-scorecard.
metadata:
  category: developer
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-scorecard

Create a decision scorecard for test strategies: a weighted comparison for choosing between options. Produces a copy-ready scorecard. Triggers: "test strategies scorecard", /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-scorecard.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team wants to grade its current testing practice against an explicit rubric using CI data and the tests themselves as evidence - before expanding agent involvement in test writing, after a painful escape, or as a recurring health check. The scorecard's distinguishing rule: no dimension is graded from the team's self-description. Shape comes from CI minutes, flake from retry data, assertion quality from reading (and ideally mutating) real tests.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- CI data: runtime and pass/retry history per pipeline, test counts by level if derivable
- A sample of actual test files across levels, including recent agent-written tests if any
- Escaped-defect list for the period, with modules and fix PRs
- The team's flake policy and coverage policy as written (or the admission that none exists)
- Recent PRs where tests were deleted, skipped, or modified, for the integrity dimension

## Workflow

1. Fix the sample and method: which pipelines, which test files, what period - and state which dimensions were graded by measurement, by reading, or provisionally from description.
2. Score each dimension 0-3 with anchored definitions. **Shape sanity** (0 = CI minutes dominated by the slowest level with no rationale, 3 = shape matches the system's defect boundaries and the team can say why). **Determinism & flake policy** (0 = retry-until-green is the de facto policy, 3 = quarantine with a fix SLA, retry data monitored, quarantine queue short and young). **Assertion quality** (0 = sampled tests execute code with trivial or no assertions; snapshot updates rubber-stamped, 3 = sampled tests fail when behavior is deliberately broken - verified by a mutation spot-check on at least one module). **Feedback speed** (0 = developers batch pushes to avoid the suite, 3 = pre-merge signal fast enough that pushing small is normal). **Coverage honesty** (0 = aggregate percentage worshipped or gamed with assertion-free tests, 3 = changed-line coverage used as a floor-finder, gaps triaged by risk, no percentage targets in incentives). **Test integrity** (0 = tests deleted, skipped, or weakened to get green without stated reasons - including by agents, 3 = test modifications carry reasons, are reviewed as code, and agent-written tests get assertion-level review).
3. Weight by the team's exposure: agent-heavy test authorship up-weights integrity and assertion quality; a slow-release-cycle product may down-weight feedback speed relative to escape prevention.
4. Gather evidence per dimension: the CI-minute breakdown, the retry-rate figures, the named test files read, the mutation spot-check result, the PRs with unexplained test deletions. Every score line carries its evidence reference.
5. Apply the critical-failure override: a mutation spot-check showing sampled "covered" tests catch nothing, or a pattern of unexplained test deletions/weakenings, caps the overall grade regardless of shape and speed - a fast, green, assertion-free suite is the most dangerous configuration because it looks healthy.
6. Diagnose the weakest dimension mechanically and propose exactly one fix, preferring controls over exhortation: a quarantine workflow with an SLA, a required-reason rule for test deletions enforced in review, an assertion-review checklist for agent-written tests.
7. Set the re-score: same rubric and sampling method, dated, ideally after the fix has had one full cycle to show up in CI data.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - what practice is being graded and the decision it informs (e.g. agent test-writing scope).

**Sample & method** - pipelines, files, period; measured vs read vs provisional dimensions flagged.

**Rubric & scores** - table: dimension, weight, 0/3 anchors, score, evidence reference (CI figure, file, PR).

**Critical-failure flags** - assertion-free coverage or unexplained test removals, stated plainly.

**Overall grade** - weighted score with a one-line verdict tied to the pending decision.

**Weakest dimension deep dive** - mechanism plus the specific evidence (the retried test, the gutted assertion).

**One fix** - a control with an owner.

**Risks / assumptions** - sampling limits, mutation-check scope, period effects.

**Next action** - re-score date and method.

## Do

- Derive shape from CI minutes and counts; grade the suite the pipeline runs, not the one the wiki describes
- Run the mutation spot-check before scoring assertion quality above 1 - deliberately break behavior and watch whether tests notice
- Read agent-written tests at the assertion level; volume and green-ness are not evidence of verification
- Let retry data overrule the flake register when they disagree

## Don't

- Don't grade coverage honesty by the coverage number; grade it by what the covering tests assert and how the number is used
- Don't average away a critical failure on assertion quality or integrity - a green suite that catches nothing scores as unsafe, whatever its speed
- Don't accept "we deleted it because it was flaky" without the retry data and a stated replacement or reason
- Don't propose a testing-culture initiative as the fix; name one control, one owner, one re-score

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Every dimension's evidence source is labeled measured, read, or provisional
- [ ] Shape scores derive from CI-minute data, not self-description
- [ ] Assertion quality above 1 is backed by a mutation spot-check or explicitly provisional
- [ ] Test deletions/skips/weakenings were audited, with agent-authored cases noted
- [ ] Critical-failure override applied where warranted; one control fix with owner and dated re-score
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-scorecard or describe the task from the skill description.

name: developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-experiment-plan
description: >
  Design an experiment for test strategies: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "test strategies experiment-plan", /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-experiment-plan.
metadata:
  category: developer
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-experiment-plan

Design an experiment for test strategies: a measurable test plan with success criteria. Produces a copy-ready experiment plan. Triggers: "test strategies experiment-plan", /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-experiment-plan.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team wants to trial a test-strategy change on a bounded slice before rolling it out: replacing a band of slow E2E tests with contract tests, introducing a flaky-test quarantine policy with a fix SLA, adding a changed-lines coverage gate, or letting agents write tests under rules. The pilot must measure confidence, not vanity - the question is whether the change catches more real defects per minute of CI and developer time, not whether a coverage number went up.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- Current suite facts: runtime, pass rate, retry/re-run frequency, known flaky tests, coverage if tracked
- Escaped-defect history: bugs that reached production in the last quarter or two, and what test would plausibly have caught each
- The specific change under test, stated precisely (which E2E band, what quarantine SLA, what gate threshold)
- Pilot scope candidates: a service, package, or team with clear boundaries
- CI budget and who owns test infrastructure decisions

## Workflow

1. Baseline the suite where the pilot will run: wall-clock runtime, first-attempt pass rate, retry rate (retries are the honest flake measure - teams that retry-until-green stop seeing red), and the escaped-defect list mapped to the test level that should have caught each one.
2. State the change under test precisely enough to falsify: not "improve integration testing" but "replace these 14 checkout E2E scenarios with consumer-driven contract tests plus 3 retained smoke E2Es, in the payments service, for eight weeks."
3. Predict the mechanism: which baseline problem the change attacks (E2E flake burning trust, slow feedback, escapes in cross-service contracts) - so the pilot measures that mechanism, not everything at once.
4. Choose confidence-anchored metrics: escaped defects in the pilot scope, defects caught pre-merge (with test level), time-to-green on a typical PR, first-attempt pass rate, quarantine queue size and age if piloting a flake policy. Explicitly exclude aggregate coverage percentage as a success metric; track it only as a guardrail against large drops.
5. Set integrity rules for the pilot, especially where agents write or modify tests: deleted or skipped tests require a stated reason in the PR; assertions may not be weakened to make suites pass; snapshot-test updates require a human actually reading the new snapshot. Violations are pilot findings, not footnotes.
6. Define the stop rule: an escaped defect that the removed/replaced tests would demonstrably have caught halts the rollback-able part of the pilot immediately for analysis.
7. Plan the comparison honestly: same-scope history as baseline, a parallel non-pilot service as rough control, and a defect-mapping review at the midpoint and end - with the sample-size caveat stated (defects are rare events; the pilot may need qualitative review verdicts alongside counts).
8. Set decision criteria: adopt and extend, adjust (e.g. different E2E retention set), or revert - and who decides.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - the strategy change and the confidence question it must answer.

**Baseline** - runtime, first-attempt pass rate, retry rate, escaped-defect mapping for the pilot scope.

**Change under test** - the falsifiable statement of exactly what changes, where, for how long.

**Mechanism & prediction** - which baseline problem this attacks and what should move.

**Metrics** - table: metric, how collected, baseline, target; coverage listed only as a guardrail.

**Integrity rules** - test deletion/skip/weakening policy during the pilot, especially for agent-written tests.

**Stop rule** - the escaped-defect halt condition.

**Comparison plan** - historical baseline, rough control, midpoint and endpoint defect-mapping reviews.

**Decision criteria** - adopt / adjust / revert, with the deciding owner.

**Risks / assumptions** - rare-event sample sizes, seasonal load differences, Hawthorne effects.

**Next action** - usually the baseline retry-rate and escaped-defect mapping.

## Do

- Measure retries, not just failures - the retry button is where flake hides
- Map each historical escaped defect to the test level that should have caught it; this is the pilot's ground truth
- Keep a small retained smoke set when removing E2Es, and name exactly which scenarios it covers
- Treat any test deleted, skipped, or weakened without stated reason during the pilot as a first-class finding

## Don't

- Don't use aggregate coverage percentage as a success metric; a suite can hit 90% while asserting almost nothing
- Don't pilot two strategy changes in the same scope at once - attribution dies
- Don't let agents "fix" failing tests during the pilot by editing expectations; route those to a human with the integrity rule
- Don't declare success on zero escapes over a window too short for escapes to be expected; state the window's statistical weakness

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The change under test is stated precisely enough to falsify
- [ ] Baseline includes retry rate and an escaped-defect-to-test-level mapping
- [ ] Metrics measure confidence and feedback speed; coverage appears only as a guardrail
- [ ] Integrity rules for test deletion/weakening are explicit
- [ ] The stop rule names the escaped-defect halt condition and an owner for the decision
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-experiment-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

2. State the change under test precisely enough to falsify: not "improve integration testing" but "replace these 14 checkout E2E scenarios with consumer-driven contract tests plus 3 retained smoke E2Es, in the payments service, for eight weeks."
3. Predict the mechanism: which baseline problem the change attacks (E2E flake burning trust, slow feedback, escapes in cross-service contracts) - so the pilot measures that mechanism, not everything at once.
4. Choose confidence-anchored metrics: escaped defects in the pilot scope, defects caught pre-merge (with test level), time-to-green on a typical PR, first-attempt pass rate, quarantine queue size and age if piloting a flake policy. Explicitly exclude aggregate coverage percentage as a success metric; track it only as a guardrail against large drops.
5. Set integrity rules for the pilot, especially where agents write or modify tests: deleted or skipped tests require a stated reason in the PR; assertions may not be weakened to make suites pass; snapshot-test updates require a human actually reading the new snapshot. Violations are pilot findings, not footnotes.
6. Define the stop rule: an escaped defect that the removed/replaced tests would demonstrably have caught halts the rollback-able part of the pilot immediately for analysis.
7. Plan the comparison honestly: same-scope history as baseline, a parallel non-pilot service as rough control, and a defect-mapping review at the midpoint and end - with the sample-size caveat stated (defects are rare events; the pilot may need qualitative review verdicts alongside counts).
8. Set decision criteria: adopt and extend, adjust (e.g. different E2E retention set), or revert - and who decides.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - the strategy change and the confidence question it must answer.

**Baseline** - runtime, first-attempt pass rate, retry rate, escaped-defect mapping for the pilot scope.

**Change under test** - the falsifiable statement of exactly what changes, where, for how long.

**Mechanism & prediction** - which baseline problem this attacks and what should move.

**Metrics** - table: metric, how collected, baseline, target; coverage listed only as a guardrail.

**Integrity rules** - test deletion/skip/weakening policy during the pilot, especially for agent-written tests.

**Stop rule** - the escaped-defect halt condition.

**Comparison plan** - historical baseline, rough control, midpoint and endpoint defect-mapping reviews.

**Decision criteria** - adopt / adjust / revert, with the deciding owner.

**Risks / assumptions** - rare-event sample sizes, seasonal load differences, Hawthorne effects.

**Next action** - usually the baseline retry-rate and escaped-defect mapping.

## Do

- Measure retries, not just failures - the retry button is where flake hides
- Map each historical escaped defect to the test level that should have caught it; this is the pilot's ground truth
- Keep a small retained smoke set when removing E2Es, and name exactly which scenarios it covers
- Treat any test deleted, skipped, or weakened without stated reason during the pilot as a first-class finding

## Don't

- Don't use aggregate coverage percentage as a success metric; a suite can hit 90% while asserting almost nothing
- Don't pilot two strategy changes in the same scope at once - attribution dies
- Don't let agents "fix" failing tests during the pilot by editing expectations; route those to a human with the integrity rule
- Don't declare success on zero escapes over a window too short for escapes to be expected; state the window's statistical weakness

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] The change under test is stated precisely enough to falsify
- [ ] Baseline includes retry rate and an escaped-defect-to-test-level mapping
- [ ] Metrics measure confidence and feedback speed; coverage appears only as a guardrail
- [ ] Integrity rules for test deletion/weakening are explicit
- [ ] The stop rule names the escaped-defect halt condition and an owner for the decision

```markdown
---
name: developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-insight-report
description: >

metadata:
  category: developer
  type: premium
  tier: premium
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
  real_world_use_case: Product, Developer & Research
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-insight-report

Turn raw notes into insights for test strategies: actionable patterns from messy inputs. Produces a copy-ready insight report. Triggers: "test strategies insight-report", /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-insight-report.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT, Codex, VS Code agent skills, Gemini, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Use this when a team wants an evidence-based read on how its test suite is actually performing as a defect-catching and feedback system - using CI history, escaped-defect records, and the test diffs themselves. The report's sharpest tool is the mismatch hunt: places where the numbers say one thing (high coverage, green builds) and reality says another (escapes from "covered" modules, retry-until-green culture). If the team is choosing a new strategy rather than judging the current one, use the research-brief skill.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if the missing detail changes the answer.

- CI history for the period: pass/fail per run, retry counts, suite wall-clock times, which tests fail most
- Escaped defects: production bugs from the period, each with the module involved and the fix PR
- Coverage reports if they exist, at module granularity, with the team's honest opinion of them
- Test-diff history: PRs that added, deleted, skipped, or modified tests - flagged by author type (human/agent) if distinguishable
- The team's stated strategy (pyramid intentions, flake policy) to compare against observed behavior

## Workflow

1. Fix the evidence base: CI window, defect sources, and what is not observable (e.g. bugs users never reported).
2. Build the suite's actual shape versus its stated shape: count tests and runtime share by level (unit/integration/E2E). An "we follow the pyramid" team whose CI minutes are 70% E2E has an inverted pyramid in practice, whatever the doc says.
3. Run the mismatch hunt, module by module where data allows: modules with high coverage *and* escaped defects are the priority finding - inspect their tests for assertion-free execution (tests that run code but check nothing), over-mocked units that verify the mock, and snapshot tests that were bulk-updated without review.
4. Audit flake behavior from retries, not from the flake register: tests retried routinely are de facto quarantined without the honesty of a quarantine; measure retry rate, the top retried tests, and time lost to re-runs. Note whether red-main incidents correlate with "it was just the flaky one" dismissals.
5. Audit test-diff integrity: deleted or skipped tests and weakened assertions in the period, each with its PR and stated reason (or absence of one). Separate agent-authored changes if identifiable - agents deleting or gutting failing tests to get green is a known failure signature and deserves its own count.
6. Map each escaped defect to the cheapest test level that would plausibly have caught it; aggregate to show where the strategy underinvests (e.g. every escape is a cross-service contract issue and the suite has no contract tests).
7. Rank findings by cost (escapes and time-to-green beat style observations) and recommend two or three changes with owner and confirming signal - typically one shape change, one integrity control, one flake-policy change.

## Safety & approval

- Separate facts, assumptions, and recommendations.
- Do not invent private data, prices, policies, laws, medical facts, or platform rules.
- Before any irreversible action, produce a review table and wait for the user to say `apply`, `send`, `publish`, or another explicit approval.
- For legal, financial, health, hiring, security, or compliance topics, provide decision support only. Tell the user when a qualified professional should review the result.

## Output format

**Objective** - the question this report answers about the suite's real performance.

**Evidence reviewed** - CI window, defect sources, coverage availability, observability gaps.

**Stated vs actual shape** - test counts and CI-minute share by level, against the claimed strategy.

**Mismatch findings** - high-coverage-with-escapes modules, with what inspection of their tests revealed.

**Flake findings** - retry rate, top offenders, time lost, de facto quarantine behavior.

**Test-integrity findings** - deletions, skips, weakened assertions with PR references; agent-authored cases counted separately.

**Escape mapping** - table: escaped defect, module, cheapest test level that would have caught it.

**Recommendations** - 2-3 changes with owner and confirming signal.

**Risks / assumptions** - unreported bugs, attribution judgment calls, coverage-tool caveats.

**Next action** - the single highest-cost mismatch to fix.

## Do

- Compute the suite's shape from CI minutes and test counts, not from the team's self-description
- Open the actual test files behind high-coverage escape modules; the finding is in the assertions, not the percentage
- Count retries as flake evidence even where no flake register exists
- Give agent-authored test deletions and weakened assertions their own line - the pattern is common enough to check for by name

## Don't

- Don't report coverage percentages as quality findings without inspecting what the covering tests assert
- Don't call a test flaky without run data; developers' flake folklore misidentifies tests routinely
- Don't average suite runtime across teams or services in a way that hides one pathological pipeline
- Don't end with "write more tests"; every recommendation names a level, a module class, or a control

## Quality check (before responding)

- [ ] Stated-vs-actual shape comparison uses real CI-minute and count data
- [ ] Every mismatch finding is backed by inspection of the named tests
- [ ] Flake analysis is retry-based, with the top offenders named
- [ ] Test deletions/skips/weakenings are itemized with PRs, agent-authored ones separated
- [ ] Escape mapping assigns each defect a cheapest-catching test level
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /developer-agent-rules-test-strategy-insight-report or describe the task from the skill description.
